coredata.dc:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of iron ions and zinc ions based on dual-emission carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yulin
Lao, Siyuan
Ding, Weijie
Zhang, Zhenda
Liu, Siyu
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we developed a facile route for the preparation of dual-emission carbon dots (DCDs) by carbonizing glutathione in the water/formamide mixture. The synthesized DCDs displayed dual emission band respectively at 470 nm and 655 nm under the single-wavelength excitation at 400 nm, which can be used for the detection iron ions (Fe3+) and zinc ions (Zn2+) in different pH environments. An obvious fluorescence quenching around 655 nm was detected with the addition of Fe3+ in acidic environments, and a linear range of 2.5–30 μmol·L−1 and a detection limit of 0.8 μmol L−1 for Fe3+ were achieved. Moreover, another fluorescence quenching at 470 nm was also observed with the addition of Zn2+ in alkaline environments, and a linear range of 2.5–50 μmol L−1 and a detection limit of 1.2 μmol L−1 for Zn2+ were achieved. The prepared DCDs was used as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for determination of Fe3+ and Zn2+ ions in serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission
Carbon dots
Ratiometric
Iron ions
Zinc ions

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions based on DCDs
Detection of Zn2+ ions based on DCDs
Synthesis and characterization of the DCDs
The ratiometric fluorescent detection of Fe3+ions in acidic environments
The ratiometric fluorescent detection of Zn2+ ions based on DCDs
The application in the detection of Fe3+ and Zn2+ ions in serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the preparation of DCDs and the application in the detection of Fe3+ and Zn2+.

The fluorescence emission spectra of various carbon dots by carbonizing glutathione in the water/formamide mixture with various volume ratios at 180 ℃ for 4 h. (a: pure formamide, b: 0.5/99.5, c: 1/99, d: 2.5/97.5, e: 5/95, f: 10/90, g: 25/75, h: pure water). The excitation wavelength was set at 400 nm.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of various carbon dots by carbonizing glutathione in the water/formamide mixture with various volume ratios. (a: pure formamide, b: 0.5/99.5, c: 1/99, d: 2.5/97.5, e: 5/95, f: 10/90, g: 25/75, h: pure water). Photo: The carbon dots solution prepared by carbonizing glutathione in the water/formamide mixture with various volume ratios. (a: pure formamide, b: 0.5/99.5, c: 1/99, d: 2.5/97.5, e: 5/95, f: 10/90, g: 25/75, h: pure water) under visible light.

(A)The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of DCDs-1 (curve a represents emission at 470 nm, curve b represents emission at 655 nm, curve c represents the emission spectra upon the excitation of 400 nm) (B) TEM images and particle size distribution of prepared DCDs-1.

The fluorescence emission spectra of DCDs-1 respectively incubated with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (a: 0, b: 1, c: 2.5, d: 5, e: 10, f: 15, g: 20, h: 30, i: 40, j: 50, k: 62.5, l:75 μmol·L−1) for 5 min. Inset: the linear plots of ratio F470/F655 versus the Fe3+ ions concentration in the range from 0 to 30 μmol L−1. The excitation wavelength was set at 400 nm. The test was performed in tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 5.4). The error bars are calculated from three parallel experiments.

The fluorescence emission spectra of DCDs-1 respectively incubated with different concentrations of Zn2+ ions (a: 0, b: 2.5, c: 6.25, d: 12.5, e: 18.75, f: 25, g: 37.5, h: 50, i: 75, j: 100, k: 200, l:400 μmol·L−1) for 5 min. Inset: the linear plots of ratio F655/F470 versus the Zn2+ ions concentration in the range from 2.5 to 50 μmol L−1. The excitation wavelength was set at 400 nm. The test was performed in tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 9.4).The error bars are calculated from three parallel experiments.

head.ce:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of iron ions and zinc ions based on dual-emission carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life and Health Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life and Health Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we developed a facile route for the preparation of dual-emission carbon dots (DCDs) by carbonizing glutathione in the water/formamide mixture. The synthesized DCDs displayed dual emission band respectively at 470 nm and 655 nm under the single-wavelength excitation at 400 nm, which can be used for the detection iron ions (Fe3+) and zinc ions (Zn2+) in different pH environments. An obvious fluorescence quenching around 655 nm was detected with the addition of Fe3+ in acidic environments, and a linear range of 2.5–30 μmol·L−1 and a detection limit of 0.8 μmol L−1 for Fe3+ were achieved. Moreover, another fluorescence quenching at 470 nm was also observed with the addition of Zn2+ in alkaline environments, and a linear range of 2.5–50 μmol L−1 and a detection limit of 1.2 μmol L−1 for Zn2+ were achieved. The prepared DCDs was used as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for determination of Fe3+ and Zn2+ ions in serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past 20 years, a great number of fluorescent probes have been fabricated and successfully used for detecting various analytes, including ions, molecule, protein, and DNA [1–3]. Most of the fluorescence analyses were based on single signal changes, such as fluorescence quenching or enhancement [4,5]. However, these fluorescence analyses are susceptible to interference from precision and stability of instrument, background noises, and the concentration of fluorescence probe [6]. The ratiometric fluorescence assay with self-calibration function that are mainly based on the determination of the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two well-resolved emission peak, can effectively eliminate the external interference such as fluctuation of light source, background absorption. The key of the ratiometric fluorescence assay is always the design and preparation of dual-emission fluorescence probe [7,8]. Therefore, a series of synthetic approaches for ratiometric fluorescence probe with dual-emission have been developed [9–13].

Most ratiometric fluorescence probes are obtained by embedding or linking fluorescent material to the non-fluorescent or fluorescent matrix [14–18]. For instance, Zhuang et al. fabricated a ratiometric fluorescence biosensor for detection of hydroxyl radical based on bovine serum albumin-protected gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) and the organic molecule 2-[6-(4′-hydroxy)phenoxy-3H-xanthen-3-on-9-yl]benzoic acid as a reference fluorophore and recognition element [14]. Lu and coworkers prepared a dual-emission fluorescent probe for Cu2+ ions by encapsulating hydrophobic carbon dots (CDs) in micelles formed by self-assembly of amphiphilic polymer DSPE-PEG and tetrakis (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP)-modified DSPE-PEG [16]. Yan and coworkers designed a novel nanohybrid ratiometric fluorescence probe for determination of nitrogen dioxide that is composed of blue-colored fluorescence CDs around 460 nm and red-colored emission CdTe quantum dots (QDs) around 665 nm [18]. However, the leakage of embedded fluorescent materials from the matrixes, complex preparation and purification processes, and high cost severely restricted their further applications [8]. Hence, it is very necessary to develop a feasible route for the simple preparation of ratiometric fluorescent probes with dual emission. Up to now, the ratiometric fluorescent probes prepared by one-pot synthesis based on CDs are very rare [19,20]. Herein, we developed a simple hydrothermal route for the preparation of dual-emission carbon dots (DCDs) by directly carbonizing glutathione (GSH) in the water/formamide mixture. The synthesized DCDs exhibited two separate fluorescence emission peak respectively at 470 nm and 655 nm upon the excitation of 400 nm, which offered the potential to be a ratiometric fluorescent probe.

Iron ion (Fe3+) is a kind of heavy metal ion, which plays an important role in both organism and environment system, and is associated with a series of physiological disorders including heart disease, liver and renal injury, anemia, etc [21,22]. Zinc, occurring as a divalent cation Zn2+ ions, is an essential trace element in human body [23]. Zn2+ ion can sustain series of human activities including DNA and peptide synthesis, RNA transcription, cell apoptosis, metalloenzyme regulation, neurophysiology, etc [24,25]. Therefore, various fluorescent probes were used for the determination of Fe3+ and Zn2+ ions concentrations [21,26–28]. For example, Wang’s team employed adenosine 5′-triphosphate (ATP) as the carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus source to prepare the ultra-bright N/P co-doped CDs, whose fluorescence was sensitive toward Fe3+ ions in a linear range from 1 to 150 μmol L−1 [21]. Hashemi and coworkers developed a novel water-soluble iron quantum clusters incorporated into hemoglobin, and Zn2+ ions could induce a visible color changes and fluorescence “turn on” of the iron quantum clusters solution [27]. However, there have been rare reports about the ratiometric fluorescence probe for synchronous detection of Fe3+ and Zn2+ ions based on the DCDs.

Herein, the fluorescence emission band at 655 nm of the prepared DCDs displayed an obvious quenching with the addition of Fe3+ ions in acidic environments, and the emission band at 470 nm exhibited an obvious quenching with the addition of Zn2+ ions in alkaline environments. Therefore, the prepared DCDs can be used as a ratiometric probe for fluorescent detection of Fe3+ and Zn2+ in different pH environments.

GSH, formamide, adenosine triphosphate (ATP), adenosine diphosphate (ADP), adenosine monophosphate (AMP) were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation. Tris, hydrochloric acid (HCl), ferric chloride, zinc chloride and other metal salts were all obtained from Bejing Dingguo Genetech Co., Ltd, and were of analytical reagent grade. Tris-HCl buffer solution was used to keep the pH environment of the system. The deionized water used in the experiments offered a resistivity higher than 18 MΩcm−1.

The fluorescence and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded on a TECAN infinite M100 PRO Biotek microplate reader. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) was obtained using a BrukerIFS66 V FT-IR spectrometer. The zeta potential measurements were finished with a Malvern Nano ZS90 instrument. The morphology and size of the prepared various carbon dots were observed by JEM-ARM200 F Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM samples were prepared by dropping the aqueous solution containing carbon dots onto carbon-coated copper grids and allowing the excess solvent to evaporate.

The DCDs were prepared d by one-pot synthesis, which can be described as following: 92 mg glutathione was dissolved in 10 mL of the water/formamide mixture at room temperature, and the volume ratio of deionized water to formamide was setting at 2.5/97.5. The obtained mixture was transferred into a 25 mL teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, which was then kept in an air-circulating oven at 180 ℃ for 4 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was naturally cooled down to room temperature. The obtained brown solution was centrifuged at the speed of 11, 000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles. The upper solution was loaded in a dialysis tubing with a molecular weight cut of 3KD against ultrapure water to remove some free molecules. The purified DCDs solution was freeze-dried into powder. The concentration of purified DCDs solution used in the following process was about 4.6 mg mL−1 as measured by the dry weight analysis method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions based on DCDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of Fe3+ ions, 20 μL of purified DCDs solution (4.6 mg mL−1) and varying amounts of ferric chloride solution were separately added into the mixture of 200 μL of tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mmol·L-1, pH 5.4). Then the mixture was thoroughly shaken and equilibrated for 5 min. The fluorescence spectra and UV–vis spectroscopy were recorded and used for quantitative analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Zn2+ ions based on DCDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of Zn2+ ions, 20 μL of purified DCDs solution (4.6 mg mL−1) and varying amounts of zinc chloride solution were separately added into the mixture of 200 μL of tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mmol·L-1, pH 9.4). Then the mixture was thoroughly shaken and equilibrated for 5 min. The fluorescence spectra and UV–vis spectroscopy were recorded and used for quantitative analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the DCDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in scheme, the DCDs can be directly prepared by carbonizing GSH in the water/formamide mixture. In order to investigate the generation of DCDs, we prepared series of carbon dots by carbonizing GSH in the mixture of water and formamide with different proportions at 180℃. Fig. 1 provides the fluorescence emission spectra of various CDs prepared by carbonizing glutathione in the water/formamide mixture with different volume ratios (Scheme 1 ). It can be found that the CDs directly prepared by carbonizing GSH in pure formamide (defined as R-CDs) exhibited an obvious fluorescence emission peak around 685 nm and a very weak peak around 470 nm (Fig. 1 curve a), which was similar to previous report [29]. As shown in Fig. 1, the changes of water amount can have major impact on the fluorescence of the prepared CDs. The fluorescence emission peak at 685 nm showed an obvious blue shift from 685 nm to 655 nm, and the fluorescence emission peak at 470 nm gradually enhanced as the ratio of water/formamide increased from 0 to 2.5/97.5. The fluorescence emission peaks at 655 nm and 470 nm both decreased with the increase of water/formamide ratios from 2.5/97.5 to 25/75. Only the fluorescence emission peak at 470 nm can be observed after the water/formamide ratio is higher than 10/90. The CDs prepared by carbonizing GSH in pure water (defined as G-CDs) exhibited only a single fluorescence emission peak around 470 nm (Fig. 1curve h). The results indicated that water molecules play a key role in controlling the dual emission peaks of DCDs, and a little water may be beneficial to the generation of DCDs. Fig. 2 provides the UV–vis absorption spectra of various CDs prepared by carbonizing GSH in the water/formamide mixture with different volume ratios. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of R-CDs displayed several characteristic absorbing peaks respectively at 395 nm, 420 nm, 640 nm and 680 nm, which can be attributed to n→π* and π→π* transitions of the aromatic π system containing the CO, CN, and CS bonds [7,29]. As shown in Fig. 2, these characteristic absorbing peaks at 395 nm and 680 nm gradually weakened with the increase of water/formamide ratios from 0 to 2.5/97.5. Furthermore, the solutions colors changed from deep green to colorless transparent with the increase of water/formamide ratios (Fig. 2photo). The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the dual emission CDs prepared by carbonizing GSH in the water/formamide mixture with the proportions of 2.5/ 97.5 (defined as DCDs-1) were provided in Fig. 3 A. It can be found that the DCDs-1 offered two different excitation band respectively around 360 nm (emission peak at 470 nm) and 420 nm (emission peak at 655 nm), and there are some overlap between the two excitation band. Furthermore, two obvious emission peaks around 470 nm and 680 nm can be observed with the single excitation wavelength of 400 nm. Therefore, the prepared DCDs-1 has the potential to be a ratiometric fluorescent probe. So far, the reported ratiometric fluorescent probes that consisted of CDs were mainly based on the mixture or link of two different fluorescent CDs [15,19,20]. In order to confirm the two emission peaks at 470 nm and 655 nm of DCDs-1 are from one CDs, rather than a mixture of two kinds of fluorescent CDs, we compared the fluorescent and absorption performance of R-CDs, G-CDs, the mixture of R-CDs and G-CDs, and DCDs-1. As shown in Fig. S1A, the mixed solution of R-CDs and G-CDs with a volume ratio of 97.5/2.5 displayed an intense fluorescent emission peak at 685 nm and a weak emission peak at 470 nm that should respectively belong to R-CDs and G-CDs. The fluorescence emission spectra of DCD-1 (Fig. S1A curve d) are entirely different from the mixture of R-CDs and G-CDs (curve c) including emission peak position and fluorescence intensity. Fig. S1B showed that the DCDs-1 displayed two characteristic absorbing peaks respectively at 420 nm and 640 nm, which has great difference from the absorption spectra of R-CDs, G-CDs and the mixture of R-CDs and G-CDs. Therefore, the two emission peaks of DCDs-1 are not from the mixture of two types of CDs with different emission wavelengths. Herein, we prepared a novel kind of dual-emission CDs.

The size and morphologies of prepared DCDs-1, G-CDs and R-CDs were observed by TEM. Fig. 3B indicated that the DCDs-1 had a size distribution from 1.86 to 6.29 nm and an average size of 3.61 nm. As shown in Fig. S2A, the G-CDs prepared in pure water had a spherical shape and a size distribution in the range of 2.54–6.24 nm with an average size of 4.16 nm. Compared to G-CDs and DCDs-1, the R-CDs prepared in pure formamide offered a narrower size distribution in the range from 1.93 to 4.31 nm and a smaller average size of 3.03 nm (Fig. S2B). Fig. S3 describes the zeta potential changes of G-CDs DCDs-1 and R-CDs. The zeta potential of the G-CDs was determined to be + 7.1 mV, and the R-CDs and DCDs-1 exhibited strong negative charge on their surfaces. The obvious change of zeta potential can be attributed to the variations of functional groups on their surface. FT-IR spectrum of GSH, RCDs, DCDs-1 and G-CDs were provided to characterize the surface functional groups in Fig. S4. The broad peaks from 3000-3500 cm−1 attributed to OH and NH stretching vibrations, the peaks at 1710 cm-1 ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO in COOH groups, and the peaks at 2940 and 1395 cm-1 related to the C–H and C–N bond stretching vibrations were all observed in Fig. S4 curve a. Compared to glutathione, the peak around 1590 cm-1 attributed to the CN/CC stretching vibrations of R-CDs, DCDs-1 and G-CDs greatly enhanced. As shown in Fig. S4, the DCDs-1 and R-CDs exhibited the stronger FT-IR absorption peaks at 1710 cm−1 than G-CDs, which indicated the prepared R-CDs and DCDs-1 had more rich COOH groups on their surface. A new the peak at 2350 cm-1 attributed to C CC/CC bond appeared in the curve c, which illustrated that the DCDs-1 samples exhibited more severe decarboxylation and carbonization.

Before the further application of DCDs-1 as probe in sensing, the stability of DCDs-1 in many kinds of environments was tested. Herein, we respectively investigated the influence of ionic strength, various molecules, pH environments and sustained xenon arc light exposure on the fluorescence of DCDs-1. Fig. S5 respectively described the fluorescence emission intensity at 470 nm (F470) and 655 nm (F655) of DCDs-1 incubated with increasing KCl or NaCl concentration from 0 to 500 mmol·L−1 for 30 min. The results illustrated that even high ionic strength had a negligible effect on the two peaks F470 and F655 of the DCDs-1. Furthermore, we investigated the interference of various molecules and proteins including GSH, ATP, glucose (Glu), urea, histidine (His), arginine (Arg), glycine (Gly), bovine serum albumin (BSA), lysozyme (Lyz), trypsin (Try), pepsin (Pep) on the fluorescence of DCDs-1. As described in Fig. S6, both of the fluorescence peak F470 and F655 displayed the high stability incubated with most common molecules and proteins. Based on some previous reports, the fluorescent carbon dots are easily affected by the changes of pH environments [30,31]. We further investigated the influence of various pH environments (pH 3.0 to 10.2) on the fluorescence of DCDs-1. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity F470 and F655 of DCDs-1 can both remain the same even in some extreme pH environments, which indicated the potential application in a wide pH environment. In order to test the photostability of DCDs-1, we utilized a xenon lamp for continuous irradiation at 400 nm for 5400 s. The photobleaching curves of the DCDs-1 were offered in Fig. S8, we can find that the fluorescence emission peak at 470 and 655 nm of the DCDs-1 were almost invariable during the irradiation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric fluorescent detection of Fe3+ions in acidic environments
ce:section.ce:para:
Due to the abundant functional groups on the surface of carbon dots, series of carbon dots were used as a fluorescent probe for metal ions detection [21,26–28,32,33]. Therefore, we further discussed the possibility of the DCDs-1as a probe for the fluorescence detection of metal ions. We systematically investigated the influence of a series of metal ions including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Cu2+ or Fe3+ on the fluorescence of DCDs-1 (Fig. S9). We found that only Fe3+ ions can quench the fluorescence emission peak at 655 nm significantly, and the fluorescence emission peak at 470 nm only changed a little in the presence of Fe3+ ions (Fig. S8A). As illustrated in Fig. S8B, the fluorescence intensity ratio F470/F655 increased 2.5 folds after incubation with 30 μmol L−1 Fe3+ ions, and the ratio F470/F655 response to other metal ions was little even in the presence of 25-fold concentration of other metal ions. The selective response to Fe3+ ions indicated that the prepared DCDs-1 has the potential to be a novel ratiometric probe for detection of Fe3+ ions. And then we studied the effects of various Fe3+ ions concentrations on the DCDs-1 in detail. Fig. 4 A describes the fluorescence emission spectra of DCDs-1 with various concentrations of Fe3+ ions. It can be seen that the fluorescence emission intensity at 655 nm (F655) of DCDs-1 dramatically decreased with the increase of Fe3+ ions concentration, and the fluorescence emission intensity at 470 nm (F470) decreased slightly as Fe3+ ions concentration increased. Fig. 4 Inset indicates the ratio F470/F655 and Fe3+ ions concentration keeps a linear relationship in the range from 2.5 to 30 μmol L-1. The regression equation can be described as:
(1) F470/F655 = 0.00716[Fe3+] + 0.49744

The regression coefficient is 0.990, and the detection limit (LOD) for Fe3+ ions is 0.8 μmol L−1 calculated from the 3σ IUPAC criteria (LOD = 3S/b, S represents the standard deviation of the blank solution gained by 10 times, b represents the slope of the equation). Fig. S10 offered the photograph of DCDs-1 solution respectively incubated with various concentrations of Fe3+ ions under UV lamp irradiation (365 nm). With the increase of Fe3+ ions concentrations, the DCDs-1 solutions displays a fluorescence color change from cyan to blue violet, which is available for the visual detection of Fe3+ by the naked eye. Therefore, the prepared DCDs-1 can be used as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for selective detection of Fe3+ ions. We further summarized the performance of previous fluorescence sensing systems for Fe3+ ions detection in Table S1. Compared to the previous reports, we proposed a simple ratiometric fluorescent assay for Fe3+ ions with superior or similar dynamic range and detection limit. Furthermore, the prepared DCDs-1 had well selectivity for Fe3+ ions detection over other metal ions. Fig. S11 indicated that Fe3+ ion exhibited a wide broad absorption band in the range from 250 to 500 nm, and there was a broad overlap with the excitation bands of DCDs-1. Fig. S12 describes the UV–vis absorption spectra of DCDs-1 respectively incubated with different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. It can be found that the absorbance of DCDs-1 solutions rose gradually with the increase of Fe3+ ions concentrations. As shown in Fig. S11, the absorption of Fe3+ ions covers well the fluorescent excitation band with emission at 470 nm. Therefore, the slight decrease of fluorescence emission intensity at 470 nm of DCDs-1 could be attributed to the fluorescence inner filter effect between Fe3+ and DCDs-1 [28,34]. Figs. 3 and S11 also showed that the fluorescent excitation band with emission at 655 nm and emission peak at 655 nm only offered a little overlap with the absorption of Fe3+ ions, so the obvious fluorescence quenching at 655 nm induced by Fe3+ ions cannot be attributed to the inner filter effect. As shown in Fig. S12, the absorption peaks at 420 nm and 640 nm showed a little red-shift with the increase of Fe3+ ion concentrations, which can be attributed to the electronic effect of the surface groups [35]. According to some previous reports, the excited electrons of red emitting carbon dots can be effectively transferred from the excited state to the half-filled 3d-orbital of Fe3+ ions, and the non-radiative electron/hole annihilation resulted in fluorescence quenching at 655 nm of DCDs-1 [35,36].

Fig. S13 shows the zeta potential changes of DCDs-1 solution with incubation of various Fe3+ concentrations. It can be found that the zeta potential of the DCDs-1 solution was determined to be -7.61 mV, and Fe3+ ions can be quickly absorbed on to the surface of DCDs-1 to neutralize the negative charges. The high affinity for Fe3+ ions of phosphate ions has been widely studied [21,37]. We further investigated various kinds of phosphate-containing molecules including ATP, ADP and AMP on the effects of Fe3+-quenched DCDs-1 systems. Fig. S14 offers the fluorescence changes of Fe3+-quenched DCDs-1 systems response to various concentrations of ATP, ADP and AMP. The results indicated the fluorescence of DCDs-1 quenched by Fe3+ gradually recovered with the addition of ATP, ADP or AMP due to the formation of Fe-O-P bonds between Fe3+ and phosphate-containing molecules, and the recovering ability followed the order ATP > ADP > AMP that was consistent with the numbers of phosphate groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric fluorescent detection of Zn2+ ions based on DCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
As described in Fig. S15, we further investigated the effects of the pH environments on the DCDs-1 solutions quenched by Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence emission peak at 655 nm of DCDs-1 solution incubated with Fe3+ exhibited an obvious fluorescence quenching in acidic pH environments, and only a slight fluorescence quenching in alkaline pH environments. Therefore, the ions-induced fluorescent changes may be influenced by pH environment. As shown in Fig. S16, we further investigated influence of a series of metal ions including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Pb2+ and Fe3+ on the DCDs-1 in alkaline environments. We found that most of cations had a minimal impact on the fluorescence emission peak at 655 nm in alkaline conditions, and Zn2+ ions can significantly quench the fluorescence emission peak at 470 nm. Fig. S16 illustrated that the fluorescence emission intensity ratio F655/F470 increased from 1.95 to 3.45 after the addition of Zn2+ ions. Fig. S17 displays the ratio F655/F470 changes of DCDs-1 with or without Zn2+ ions in various pH environments. It could be found that the ratio F655/F470 of DCDs-1 gradually increased with increase of pH from 3.0 to 10.2, so DCDs-1 may be also used as a ratiometric probe for Zn2+ ions detection in alklaine environments. We systematically investigated the influence of various concentrations of Zn2+ ions on the DCDs-1. As shown in Fig. 5 , fluorescence emission peak F470 of DCDs-1 exhibited a dramatically decrease as the concentration of Zn2+ ions rose, while the fluorescence emission peak F655 changed very little as the concentration of Zn2+ ions rose. Fig. 5 Inset indicated there existed a linear relationship between the ratio F655/F470 and Zn2+ ions concentration in the range from 2.5 to 50 μmol L−1. The regression equation can be described as:
(2) F655/F470 = 0.02886 [Zn2+] + 2.11749

The regression coefficient is 0.996, and the detection limit for Zn2+ ions is 1.2 μmol L−1 calculated from the 3σ IUPAC criteria (LOD = 3S/b, S represents the standard deviation of the blank solution gained by 10 times, b represents the slope of the equation). Fig. S18 offered the photograph of DCDs-1 solution with various concentrations of Zn2+ ions under UV lamp irradiation. With the increase of Zn2+ ions concentrations, the DCDs-1 solution displays a fluorescence color change from cyan to yellow green. As illustrated in Table S2, we summarized the previous reports about fluorescent assay for Zn2+ ions. Compared to the previous reports, we proposed a simple and convenient route to prepare a Zn2+ ions probe without complicated steps and high cost, which offered a superior or similar dynamic range and detection limit for Zn2+ ions.

Fig. S19 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of DCDs-1 respectively incubated with 0, 25, 50, 100, 200 μmol L−1 Zn2+ ions. Compared with Fe3+-induced absorbance enhancement of DCDs-1, and Zn2+ ions had little impact on the absorption spectra of DCDs-1. The zeta potential measurements indicated that Zn2+ ions can be also quickly absorbed on to the surface of DCDs-1 to neutralize the negative charges (Fig. S20). Fig. S21 revealed that the energy transfer from DCDs-1 to Zn2+ ions can be ruled out because of no obvious spectra overlap between the absorption of Zn2+ ions and the excitation and emission bands of DCDs-1. Zn2+-induced fluorescence quenching in alkaline environments could be attributed to the effective chelation with the nitrogen and oxygen atom of DCDs-1 and subsequent non-radiative electron/hole recombination annihilation through the electron transfer process [38–40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application in the detection of Fe3+ and Zn2+ ions in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the application of the proposed method in the actual samples, the prepared DCDs-1 was used for the determination of Fe3+ or Zn2+ ions in fetal bovine serum samples. The commercial fetal calf serum was firstly centrifuged at 7400 g for 25 min with a centrifugal filter unit (MWCO 3 KD) to remove some macromolecules. Different amount of Fe3+ or Zn2+ ions were added into the serum sample solution that was diluted 25 folds with deionzed water to obtain the spiked samples. Table S3 and Table S4 offered the test results obtained by standard addition method, which indicated that the recoveries of Fe3+ or Zn2+ ions detected in the serum sample solution were between 93 and 107%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was no more than 4.7%. The above results demonstrated the potential applicability of the method for the determination of Fe3+or Zn2+ ions in serum samples.

In summary, we prepared DCDs by directly carbonizing glutathione in the water /formamide mixture. The as-prepared DCDs exhibited excellent chemical and optical stabilities. The prepared DCDs can be used for determination of Fe3+ ions in acidic environments based on the obvious fluorescence quenching around 655 nm, and for determination of Zn2+ ions in alkaline environments based on the fluorescence quenching around 470 nm.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Liao Ning Province (L201501145) and College Students’ Innovation Entrepreneurship Training Program of Northeastern University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.12.139.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yulin Wang is currently pursuing his bachelor degree in bioengineering at Northeastern University, China. His research focuses on the synthesis nanomaterial and their application on biosensors.
Siyuan Lao is currently pursuing his bachelor degree in bioengineering at Northeastern University, China. His research focuses on the synthesis nanomaterial and their application on biosensors.

Weijie Ding is currently pursuing her bachelor degree in bioengineering at Northeastern University, China. Her research focuses on the synthesis fluorescent nanomaterial and their application on biosensors.

Zhenda Zhang is currently pursuing his bachelor degree in bioengineering at Northeastern University, China. His research focuses on the synthesis fluorescent nanomaterial and their application on biosensors.

Siyu Liu received his doctor degree from Jilin University at 2014. Presently, he is alecturer at College of Life and Health Sciences, Northeastern University, China. Hisresearch focuses on the fluorescence analysis and imaging based on nanomaterial.
